source_paper_title: Linear Program-Based Policies for Restless Bandits: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for (Exponentially Fast) Asymptotic Optimality
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Gast_2024, title={Linear Program-Based Policies for Restless Bandits: Necessary and Sufficient Conditions for (Exponentially Fast) Asymptotic Optimality}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0101}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0101}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Gast, Nicolas and Gaujal, Bruno and Yan, Chen}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2468–2491} }
context:
Section 4.3.2 (page 16–18, around the end of the section containing 'We actually conjecture that ...')

We actually conjecture that for “essentially all” degenerate RBs, if \(\pi\) is a LP-compatible policy, then there exists constants \(C, C>0\) independent of \(N\) such that
\[\frac{C}{\sqrt{N}} \le \bigl|V^{(N)}_{\pi}(m(0), T) - V_{\mathrm{rel}}(m(0), T)\bigr| \le \frac{C}{\sqrt{N}}.\]
This implies that as long as the RB is degenerate, we can not expect a faster than square root convergence to the LP relaxation bound, for any admissible policy.
title: Prove square-root lower bound for LP-compatible policies in degenerate restless bandits
background_latex:
Consider a discrete-time finite-horizon restless bandit with a population of \(N\) statistically identical arms. Each arm has a finite state space \(S=\{1,\dots,d\}\) and two actions \(a\in\{0,1\}\) (passive/active). If an arm is in state \(s\) and action \(a\) is chosen, an immediate reward \(R_s^a\in\mathbb{R}\) is earned and the arm transitions to state \(s'\) with probability \(P^{a}_{s,s'}\). Arms evolve independently conditional on actions.

At each time \(t=0,1,\dots,T-1\), exactly a fraction \(\alpha\in(0,1)\) of arms is activated (with the convention that if \(\alpha N\) is not an integer, the policy randomizes between \(\lfloor \alpha N\rfloor\) and \(\lfloor \alpha N\rfloor+1\) active arms so that the expected number of active arms equals \(\alpha N\)). Let \(M^{(N)}(t)\in\Delta_d\) be the empirical distribution of arm states at time \(t\), where \(M^{(N)}_s(t)\) is the fraction of arms in state \(s\). Let \(Y^{(N)}(t)\in\Delta_{2d}\) be the empirical state-action fractions, where \(Y^{(N)}_{s,a}(t)\) is the fraction of arms in state \(s\) assigned action \(a\) at time \(t\). The per-arm reward of a policy \(\pi\) is
\[V^{(N)}_{\pi}(m(0),T):=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Big[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\sum_{s\in S}\sum_{a\in\{0,1\}} R_s^a\,Y^{(N)}_{s,a}(t)\Big].\]

The standard LP relaxation replaces the hard per-time activation constraint by an equality constraint on expected activation. Writing \(y_{s,a}(t):=\mathbb{E}[Y^{(N)}_{s,a}(t)]\), the relaxation value \(V_{\mathrm{rel}}(m(0),T)\) is the optimum of
\[\max_{y\ge 0}\ \sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\sum_{s,a} R_s^a\,y_{s,a}(t)\]
subject to flow conservation
\[y_{s,0}(t+1)+y_{s,1}(t+1)=\sum_{s'\in S}\sum_{a\in\{0,1\}} y_{s',a}(t)P^{a}_{s',s}\quad \forall s,\ t,\]
activation budget
\[\sum_{s\in S} y_{s,1}(t)=\alpha\quad\forall t,\]
and initial condition \(y_{s,0}(0)+y_{s,1}(0)=m_s(0)\).

Call a Markovian admissible policy \(\pi=\{\pi_t\}_{t=0}^{T-1}\) a map \(\pi_t:\Delta_d\to\Delta_{2d}\) that, given state distribution \(m\), prescribes fractions \(y=\pi_t(m)\) satisfying \(y_{s,a}\ge 0\), \(\sum_a y_{s,a}=m_s\) for all \(s\), and \(\sum_s y_{s,1}=\alpha\). A policy is LP-compatible if there exists an optimal LP solution \(\{y^*(t)\}_{t=0}^{T-1}\) such that \(\pi_t(m^*(t))=y^*(t)\) for all \(t\), where \(m^*_s(t)=y^*_{s,0}(t)+y^*_{s,1}(t)\).

Let \(S_0(t):=\{s\in S: y^*_{s,0}(t)>0\text{ and } y^*_{s,1}(t)>0\}\). The model is called degenerate if for every optimal LP solution there exists at least one time \(t\) such that \(|S_0(t)|=0\), i.e., at that time the LP solution activates some states fully and leaves the rest fully passive with no state partially split between actions.

The paper provides an example showing that for some degenerate models, the LP-relaxation gap under any LP-compatible policy cannot decay faster than order \(1/\sqrt{N}\), and formulates a general conjecture that this phenomenon is typical for degenerate models.
problem_definition_latex:
For a fixed finite-horizon restless bandit instance \((S,\{P^a\}_{a\in\{0,1\}},\{R^a\}_{a\in\{0,1\}},\alpha,T,m(0))\) that is degenerate in the sense that for some time \(t\) every optimal solution \(\{y^*(\tau)\}_{\tau=0}^{T-1}\) of the LP relaxation satisfies \(|S_0(t)|=0\), determine whether the following statement holds (as conjectured in the paper):

There exist constants \(\underline C,\overline C>0\) (depending on the instance but not on \(N\)) such that for every LP-compatible admissible policy \(\pi\) and all sufficiently large \(N\),
\[ \frac{\underline C}{\sqrt{N}}\ \le\ \bigl|V^{(N)}_{\pi}(m(0),T)-V_{\mathrm{rel}}(m(0),T)\bigr|\ \le\ \frac{\overline C}{\sqrt{N}}. \]
In particular, the task is to prove or disprove the \(\Omega(1/\sqrt{N})\) lower bound on the LP-relaxation optimality gap for LP-compatible policies on (essentially all) degenerate finite-horizon restless bandits.

For a fixed finite-horizon restless bandit instance \((S,\{P^a\}_{a\in\{0,1\}},\{R^a\}_{a\in\{0,1\}},\alpha,T,m(0))\) that is degenerate in the sense that for some time \(t\) every optimal solution \(\{y^*(\tau)\}_{\tau=0}^{T-1}\) of the LP relaxation satisfies \(|S_0(t)|=0\), determine whether the following statement holds (as conjectured in the paper):

In particular, the task is to prove or disprove the \(\Omega(1/\sqrt{N})\) lower bound on the LP-relaxation optimality gap for LP-compatible policies on (essentially all) degenerate finite-horizon restless bandits.
location_in_paper: Section 4.3.2 (page 16–18, around the end of the section containing 'We actually conjecture that ...')
open_problem_quote:
We actually conjecture that for “essentially all” degenerate RBs, if \(\pi\) is a LP-compatible policy, then there exists constants \(C, C>0\) independent of \(N\) such that
\[\frac{C}{\sqrt{N}} \le \bigl|V^{(N)}_{\pi}(m(0), T) - V_{\mathrm{rel}}(m(0), T)\bigr| \le \frac{C}{\sqrt{N}}.\]
This implies that as long as the RB is degenerate, we can not expect a faster than square root convergence to the LP relaxation bound, for any admissible policy.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The conjecture concerns scaling of an optimality gap with N; for any fixed small-state model one can simulate or compute the induced finite-N performance of specific LP-compatible policies and estimate the gap numerically. Automated search over small d, T and rational transition probabilities could plausibly uncover degenerate instances that violate the claimed \(\Omega(1/\sqrt N)\) lower bound (or suggest refinements).
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects (small restless-bandit instances and LP-compatible policies/tie-breaking rules) can be represented programmatically, and a fitness signal can be based on computed/simulated gaps \(|V^{(N)}_{\pi}-V_{\mathrm{rel}}|\) over a range of N. However, verifying the universal quantifiers ('for every LP-compatible policy' and asymptotic bounds) is difficult computationally; the loop is more likely to find informative examples or sharpened conjectures than to resolve the statement.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.
